---
title: "Noise"
format: pdf
---

# General Question
Using the matrix-variate normal distribution can we define a noise component similar to that of the Banfield and Raftery noise, or the Hennig and Coretto. 

# Matrix-Variate Normal Distribution

- n is the number of subjects
- r is the number of occasions (time points)
- p is the number of attributes

$$
\phi_{r\times p}(\mathbf{X} | \mathbf{M}, \mathbf{V}, \mathbf{U}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{\frac{rp}{2}}|\mathbf{V}|^{\frac{r}{2}}|\mathbf{U}|^{\frac{p}{2}}} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}\text{tr}\left(\mathbf{V}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{M})^\top\mathbf{U}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}-\mathbf{M})\right) \right\}
$$

- M is a r x p mean matrix
- U is a r x r column covariance matrix
- V is a p x p column row covariance matrix

Note 1: that there is an identifiability issue with regard to U and V. If $k$ is a strictly positive constant, that replacing U and V by $(1/k)\mathbf{U}$ and $k\mathbf{V}$ respectively, leaves the distribution unchanged. Solutions have been proposed including setting $\text{tr}(\mathbf{U}) = r$ or $\mathbf{U}_{11} = 1$.

$$
\frac{1}{k}\mathbf{V} \otimes k\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{V} \otimes \mathbf{U}, \qquad \text{if} \ k >0
$$

Note 2: that the matrix-variate normal distribution is related to the multivariate normal distribution via the equivalence

$$
\mathbf{X} \sim \mathcal{N}_{r\times p} (\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{U}, \mathbf{V}) \iff \text{vec}({\mathbf{X}}) \sim \mathcal{N}_{rp}(\text{vec}(\mathbf{M}), \mathbf{V} \otimes \mathbf{U})
$$

## EM Updates

Let $z_{ig} = 1$ when observation $i$ belongs in group $g$, and $z_{ig} = 0$ else. Then, in the E-step we estimate the posterior $\hat{z}_{ig}$ that observation $\mathbf{X}_i$ belongs to group $g$

$$
\hat{z}_{ig} = \frac{\hat\pi_g\phi_{r\times p}\left(\mathbf{X}_i|\hat{\mathbf{M}}_g,\hat{\mathbf{V}}_g, \hat{\mathbf{U}}_g\right)}{\sum_{h=1}^G\pi_h\phi_{r\times p}\left(\mathbf{X}_i|\hat{\mathbf{M}}_h, \hat{\mathbf{V}}_h, \hat{\mathbf{U}}_h\right)}
$$

In M-step we get the maximum likelihood estimates:

$$
\begin{align}

\hat\pi_g &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig} \\

\hat{\mathbf{M}}_g &= \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig}}\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig}\mathbf{X}_i \\

\hat{\mathbf{U}}_g &= \frac{1}{p\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig}}\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig}(\mathbf{X}_i - \hat{\mathbf{M}}_g)\hat{\mathbf{V}}_g^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i - \hat{\mathbf{M}}_g)^\top \\

\hat{\mathbf{V}}_g &= \frac{1}{r\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig}}\sum_{i=1}^n\hat{z}_{ig}(\mathbf{X}_i - \hat{\mathbf{M}}_g)^\top\hat{\mathbf{U}}_g^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i - \hat{\mathbf{M}}_g)

\end{align}
$$

We can initialize $\hat{z}_{ig}$ using k-means by vectorizing the matrices first. 

Meaning we define the Mahalonobis distance as

$$
\mathcal{D}_M(\mathbf{X}_i, \mathbf{M}, \mathbf{V}, \mathbf{U}) = \text{tr}\left\{\mathbf{U}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i - \mathbf{M})\mathbf{V^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i - \mathbf{M})^\top} \right\}
$$

These will form as the foundation for our base Matrix Gaussian Mixture Model (MGMM) updates. Let us try putting that into a function!

## Noise

### Banfield and Raftery

BR-noise augments the GMM with a uniform noise component supported on the convex hull of the data. For univariate data, this simplifies to a uniform distribution over the observed data range:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{g = 1}^{G} \pi_g \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \vert \boldsymbol\mu_g, \boldsymbol\Sigma_g) + \pi_0\frac{1(x\in[x_{\min},x_{\max}])}{x_{\max}-x_{\min}}, \tag{1.1} \\
\sum_{g=0}^G\pi_g = 1, \quad \pi_0 \geq 0
$$ 
where the mixture weights satisfy $\sum_{g=0}^G \pi_g = 1$ and $\pi_0 \ge 0$.

The general multivariate formulation extends this idea to higher dimensions, $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ are replaced the convex hull of the dataset, and the uniform component’s density is constant within that region.

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{g = 1}^{G} \pi_g \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \vert \boldsymbol\mu_g, \boldsymbol\Sigma_g) + \pi_0 \ \mathcal{U}(\mathbf{x}) \tag{1.2}
$$

### Improper-k

HC-Noise replaces the bounded uniform density with a constant improper density $k$ defined over the entire space $\mathbb{R}^p$.

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{g = 1}^{G} \pi_g \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \vert \boldsymbol\mu_g, \boldsymbol\Sigma_g) + \pi_0 k \tag{1.3}
$$

Here $k$ is improper and thus is not a proper probability density since it does not integrate to 1. However, it still defines a pseudo-likelihood; 
$$
\tilde L(\theta)=\prod_{i=1}^n\left[\sum_{g=1}^G\pi_g\phi(\mathbf x_i|\boldsymbol{\mu_g}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma_g}) + \pi_0k \right]
$$ 
whose maximization will yield robust parameter estimates. Again, the background term $k$ acts as a "catch-all" component that absorbs observations poorly explained by any Gaussian cluster.

## Matrix Noise

For matrix-valued observations, the noise term should play the same role as the Banfield-Raftery or Hennig-Coretto background component: it should absorb matrices that are not well explained by any Gaussian cluster, without forcing them to belong to a particular structured component.

$$
f(\mathbf{X}) = \sum_{g=1}^{G} \pi_g \, \phi_{r\times p}(\mathbf{X} \,|\, \mathbf{M}_g, \mathbf{U}_g, \mathbf{V}_g) + \pi_0 \, h(\mathbf{X}),
\qquad \sum_{g=0}^{G} \pi_g = 1
$$

where $h(\mathbf{X})$ is a noise density and $\pi_0$ is the noise proportion.

### Improper k matrix-HC-noise

Let us define our improper noise $k$ as

$$
h(\mathbf{X}) = k, \qquad k > 0 \quad \forall \ \mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{rp}
$$

This is again going to be a pseudo-likelihood as $k$ will not integrate to 1 over the support $\mathbb{R}^{rp}$. 
Then the mixture becomes
$$
f(\mathbf{X}) = \sum_{g=1}^{G} \pi_g \, \phi_{r\times p}(\mathbf{X} \,|\, \mathbf{M}_g, \mathbf{U}_g, \mathbf{V}_g) + \pi_0 k
$$

Thus, the noise responsibility becomes
$$
\hat z_{i0} = \frac{\hat\pi_0 h(\mathbf{X}_i)}{\hat\pi_0 k + \sum_{g=1}^{G} \hat\pi_g \phi_{r\times p}(\mathbf{X}_i \,|\, \hat{\mathbf{M}}_g, \hat{\mathbf{U}}_g, \hat{\mathbf{V}}_g)}
$$

To pick $k$ we can just start by picking a small enough $k$ and then going from there.

### Uniform BR-noise

For matrix observations we may define a bounded uniform background either exactly on the convex hull of the observed matrices (in vectorised space) or approximately on a simple bounding hyper-rectangle.

Let $H = \mathrm{conv}\{\mathrm{vec}(\mathbf{X}_1),\dots,\mathrm{vec}(\mathbf{X}_n)\}\subset\mathbb{R}^{rp}$ be the convex hull of the vectorised data. The BR uniform noise is

$$
h_{\mathrm{BR}}(\mathbf{X}) = \frac{1(\mathrm{vec}(\mathbf{X})\in H)}{\mathrm{Vol}(H)}
$$
where $\mathrm{Vol}(H)$ is the $rp$-dimensional volume of the convex hull.

Because computing $\mathrm{Vol}(H)$ can be expensive in high dimensions, we could use the entrywise bounding hyper-rectangle: for each matrix entry index $(a,b)$ compute

$$
m_{ab}=\min_{i} [\mathbf{X}_i]_{ab} \qquad M_{ab}=\max_{i} [\mathbf{X}_i]_{ab}
$$
and define the axis-aligned hyper-rectangle

$$
R = \prod_{a=1}^r\prod_{b=1}^p [m_{ab}, M_{ab}] \subset \mathbb{R}^{rp}
$$
Then set the uniform noise density

$$
h_{\mathrm{BR}}^{\mathrm{box}}(\mathbf{X}) = \frac{1(\mathrm{vec}(\mathbf{X})\in R)}{\prod_{a,b}(M_{ab}-m_{ab})}
$$

## Noise Implmentation

```{r}
set.seed(123)
r <- 3; p <- 4
simulate_group <- function(n, mean_matrix, row_sd = 0.5, col_sd = 0.5) {
	row_cov <- diag(row_sd, nrow(mean_matrix))
	col_cov <- diag(col_sd, ncol(mean_matrix))
	lapply(seq_len(n), function(i) mean_matrix + row_cov %*% matrix(rnorm(r * p), r, p) %*% col_cov)
}

M1 <- matrix(1, r, p)
M2 <- matrix(-1, r, p)
x_list <- c(simulate_group(200, M1), simulate_group(100, M2))

contam <- lapply(seq_len(50), function(i) matrix(runif(r * p, -15, 15), r, p))
x_list <- c(x_list, contam)
```


### HC-noise

```{r}
fit_hc <- matrix_variate_noise_fit(x_list, g = 2, noise_type = "hc", noise_k = 1e-06, 
                                   nstart = 100, max_iter = 50, verbose = FALSE)

cat('HC fit mixing proportions:\n')
print(fit_hc$pi)
cat('\nHC cluster counts (0 indicates noise):\n')
print(table(fit_hc$cluster))
print(fit_hc$noise)
```

### BR-noise

```{r}
fit_br <- matrix_variate_noise_fit(x_list, g = 2, noise_type = "br", noise_jitter = 1e-08, 
                                   nstart = 100, max_iter = 50, verbose = FALSE)

cat('BR fit mixing proportions:\n')
print(fit_br$pi)
cat('\nBR cluster counts (0 indicates noise):\n')
print(table(fit_br$cluster))
print(fit_br$noise)
```

HC-noise recovered the two clusters and the noise proportion accurately.

BR fit largely collapsed the two true clusters into one component (component_2), leaving component_1 with very few items. Noise proportion is near the true value (~0.13), but cluster recovery failed for the two Gaussians.

Box diagnostics: per-entry limits $\approx$ +-13–15 and log-volume $\approx$ 40 nears uniform density = exp(-40) (extremely small). That tiny uniform density makes the BR background numerically negligible compared with Gaussian peaks, which can change how the EM balances components and makes results sensitive to initialization.
Likely causes for the BR behaviour

Bounding-box volume is huge (because contamination inflates min/max), so the BR uniform density is extremely small and competes poorly with Gaussians; EM may then prefer to explain almost-all data with one Gaussian and use the other Gaussian only for a handful of points.
Initialization sensitivity (kmeans nstart small) can let components merge.
BR using the simple axis-aligned box is fragile when contamination sets wide margins.

## Additional Tomarchio-Style Simulations

The first has no contamination, so the noise component should stay near zero. The second contaminates 30 randomly selected matrices by replacing one randomly chosen column in each with values from $\mathrm{Unif}(-15, 15)$.

```{r}
#| label: tomarchio_noise_setup
#| warning: false
#| message: false

generate_tomarchio_noise_means <- function() {
	M1 <- matrix(c(
		-2.60, -1.10, -0.50, -0.20,
		 1.30,  0.60,  0.30,  0.10
	), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)

	M2 <- matrix(c(
		 1.50,  1.70,  1.90,  2.20,
		-3.70, -2.70, -2.00, -1.50
	), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)

	list(M1 = M1, M2 = M2)
}

generate_tomarchio_noise_covariances <- function() {
	U1 <- matrix(c(2.0, 0.0,
	               0.0, 1.0), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
	U2 <- matrix(c(1.70, 0.5,
	               0.5, 1.30), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
	V <- matrix(c(
		1.00, 0.50, 0.25, 0.13,
		0.50, 1.00, 0.50, 0.25,
		0.25, 0.50, 1.00, 0.50,
		0.13, 0.25, 0.50, 1.00
	), nrow = 4, byrow = TRUE)

	list(U1 = make_spd(U1), U2 = make_spd(U2), V = make_spd(V))
}

simulate_tomarchio_noise_data <- function(n = 200, seed = NULL) {
	if (!is.null(seed)) {
		set.seed(seed)
	}

	means <- generate_tomarchio_noise_means()
	cov_list <- generate_tomarchio_noise_covariances()
	group_sizes <- c(floor(n / 2), n - floor(n / 2))
	true_groups <- rep(1:2, times = group_sizes)
	x_list <- vector("list", n)
	component_covs <- list(cov_list$U1, cov_list$U2)
	chol_V <- chol(cov_list$V)

	idx <- 1
	for (g in 1:2) {
		chol_U <- chol(component_covs[[g]])
		for (i in seq_len(group_sizes[g])) {
			z <- matrix(rnorm(2 * 4), nrow = 2, ncol = 4)
			x_list[[idx]] <- means[[g]] + t(chol_U) %*% z %*% chol_V
			idx <- idx + 1
		}
	}

	list(X = x_list, true_groups = true_groups)
}

contaminate_selected_columns <- function(x_list, n_contam = 30, low = -15, high = 15, seed = NULL) {
	if (!is.null(seed)) {
		set.seed(seed)
	}

	n <- length(x_list)
	if (n_contam > n) {
		stop("n_contam cannot exceed the number of matrices in x_list")
	}

	contam_idx <- sort(sample(seq_len(n), n_contam))
	contam_cols <- sample(seq_len(ncol(x_list[[1]])), n_contam, replace = TRUE)

	for (j in seq_along(contam_idx)) {
		i <- contam_idx[j]
		col_idx <- contam_cols[j]
		x_list[[i]][, col_idx] <- runif(nrow(x_list[[i]]), low, high)
	}

	list(X = x_list, contam_idx = contam_idx, contam_cols = contam_cols)
}

two_group_accuracy <- function(pred, truth) {
	if (length(pred) == 0) {
		return(NA_real_)
	}
	acc1 <- mean(pred == truth)
	acc2 <- mean(3 - pred == truth)
	max(acc1, acc2)
}

run_tomarchio_noise_simulation <- function(N_sim = 100, n = 200, contam_n = 0, verbose = TRUE) {
	metrics <- vector("list", N_sim * 2)
	metric_idx <- 1

	for (sim in seq_len(N_sim)) {
		if (verbose && sim %% 10 == 0) {
			cat(sprintf("Tomarchio noise simulation %d/%d\n", sim, N_sim))
		}

		dataset <- simulate_tomarchio_noise_data(n = n, seed = 9000 + sim)
		if (contam_n > 0) {
			tmp <- contaminate_selected_columns(
				dataset$X,
				n_contam = contam_n,
				low = -15,
				high = 15,
				seed = 12000 + sim
			)
			dataset$X <- tmp$X
			dataset$contam_idx <- tmp$contam_idx
			dataset$contam_cols <- tmp$contam_cols
		} else {
			dataset$contam_idx <- integer(0)
			dataset$contam_cols <- integer(0)
		}

		for (method in c("hc", "br")) {
			fit <- matrix_variate_noise_fit(
				x_list = dataset$X,
				g = 2,
				noise_type = method,
				noise_k = 1e-06,
				noise_jitter = 1e-08,
				nstart = 50,
				max_iter = 50,
				verbose = FALSE
			)

			keep_idx <- which(fit$cluster > 0)
			if (length(keep_idx) > 0) {
				cluster_accuracy <- two_group_accuracy(fit$cluster[keep_idx], dataset$true_groups[keep_idx])
			} else {
				cluster_accuracy <- NA_real_
			}

			noise_rate <- mean(fit$cluster == 0)
			false_noise_rate <- if (contam_n > 0) {
				clean_idx <- setdiff(seq_len(n), dataset$contam_idx)
				mean(fit$cluster[clean_idx] == 0)
			} else {
				noise_rate
			}
			contam_recall <- if (contam_n > 0) {
				mean(fit$cluster[dataset$contam_idx] == 0)
			} else {
				NA_real_
			}
			ari <- mclust::adjustedRandIndex(fit$cluster, dataset$true_groups)

			metrics[[metric_idx]] <- data.frame(
				sim = sim,
				method = method,
				noise_rate = noise_rate,
				false_noise_rate = false_noise_rate,
				contam_recall = contam_recall,
				cluster_accuracy = cluster_accuracy,
				ari = ari
			)
			metric_idx <- metric_idx + 1
		}
	}

	metrics_df <- do.call(rbind, metrics)
	method_levels <- sort(unique(metrics_df$method))

	summary_df <- data.frame(
		mean_noise_rate = sapply(method_levels, function(m) mean(metrics_df$noise_rate[metrics_df$method == m])),
		sd_noise_rate = sapply(method_levels, function(m) sd(metrics_df$noise_rate[metrics_df$method == m])),
		mean_false_noise_rate = sapply(method_levels, function(m) mean(metrics_df$false_noise_rate[metrics_df$method == m])),
		mean_contam_recall = sapply(method_levels, function(m) {
			vals <- metrics_df$contam_recall[metrics_df$method == m]
			if (all(is.na(vals))) NA_real_ else mean(vals, na.rm = TRUE)
		}),
		mean_cluster_accuracy = sapply(method_levels, function(m) mean(metrics_df$cluster_accuracy[metrics_df$method == m], na.rm = TRUE)),
		mean_ari = sapply(method_levels, function(m) mean(metrics_df$ari[metrics_df$method == m], na.rm = TRUE))
	)

	rownames(summary_df) <- method_levels

	list(
		metrics = metrics_df,
		summary = summary_df,
		contam_n = contam_n
	)
}
```

### Clean Tomarchio data

```{r}
#| label: tomarchio_noise_clean_run
#| warning: false
#| message: false

tomarchio_noise_clean_results <- run_tomarchio_noise_simulation(
	N_sim = 50,
	n = 100,
	contam_n = 0,
	verbose = TRUE
)

tomarchio_noise_clean_results$summary
```

```{r}
#| label: tomarchio_noise_contam_run
#| warning: false
#| message: false

tomarchio_noise_contam_results <- run_tomarchio_noise_simulation(
	N_sim = 50,
	n = 100,
	contam_n = 30,
	verbose = TRUE
)

tomarchio_noise_contam_results$summary
```

## Viroli-Style

The clean version has no contamination. The contaminated version selects 15 matrices at random and randomly permutes the entries within each selected matrix to mimic data-entry errors.

```{r}
#| label: viroli_noise_setup
#| warning: false
#| message: false

generate_viroli_noise_means <- function() {
	M1 <- matrix(0, nrow = 3, ncol = 5)
	M1[1, 1] <- 0.5
	M1[2, 1] <- 0.5

	M2 <- matrix(0, nrow = 3, ncol = 5)

	M3 <- matrix(0, nrow = 3, ncol = 5)
	M3[1, 1] <- -0.5
	M3[2, 1] <- 0.5

	list(M1 = M1, M2 = M2, M3 = M3)
}

generate_viroli_noise_corrmatrix <- function(p) {
	make_spd(clusterGeneration::rcorrmatrix(p))
}

generate_viroli_noise_dataset <- function(n = 300, group_props = c(0.3, 0.4, 0.3), seed = NULL) {
	if (!is.null(seed)) {
		set.seed(seed)
	}

	means_list <- generate_viroli_noise_means()
	group_sizes <- as.integer(n * group_props)
	if (sum(group_sizes) != n) {
		stop("group_props must multiply to integer group sizes that sum to n")
	}

	true_groups <- rep(seq_along(group_sizes), times = group_sizes)
	x_list <- vector("list", n)
	U_list <- lapply(seq_along(group_sizes), function(g) generate_viroli_noise_corrmatrix(3))
	V_list <- lapply(seq_along(group_sizes), function(g) generate_viroli_noise_corrmatrix(5))

	idx <- 1
	for (g in seq_along(group_sizes)) {
		chol_U <- chol(U_list[[g]])
		chol_V <- chol(V_list[[g]])
		for (i in seq_len(group_sizes[g])) {
			z <- matrix(rnorm(3 * 5), nrow = 3, ncol = 5)
			x_list[[idx]] <- means_list[[g]] + t(chol_U) %*% z %*% chol_V
			idx <- idx + 1
		}
	}

	list(
		X = x_list,
		true_groups = true_groups,
		group_sizes = group_sizes,
		U_list = U_list,
		V_list = V_list
	)
}

permute_matrix_entries <- function(x) {
	matrix(sample(as.vector(x)), nrow = nrow(x), ncol = ncol(x))
}

contaminate_viroli_matrices <- function(x_list, n_contam = 15, seed = NULL) {
	if (!is.null(seed)) {
		set.seed(seed)
	}

	n <- length(x_list)
	if (n_contam > n) {
		stop("n_contam cannot exceed the number of matrices in x_list")
	}

	contam_idx <- sort(sample(seq_len(n), n_contam))
	for (i in contam_idx) {
		x_list[[i]] <- permute_matrix_entries(x_list[[i]])
	}

	list(X = x_list, contam_idx = contam_idx)
}

best_three_group_accuracy <- function(pred, truth) {
	keep_idx <- which(pred > 0)
	if (length(keep_idx) == 0) {
		return(NA_real_)
	}

	pred_keep <- pred[keep_idx]
	truth_keep <- truth[keep_idx]
	perms <- list(
		c(1, 2, 3), c(1, 3, 2), c(2, 1, 3),
		c(2, 3, 1), c(3, 1, 2), c(3, 2, 1)
	)
	max(sapply(perms, function(perm) mean(perm[pred_keep] == truth_keep)))
}

run_viroli_noise_simulation <- function(N_sim = 100, n = 300, contam_n = 0, verbose = TRUE) {
	results <- vector("list", N_sim * 2)
	result_idx <- 1

	for (sim in seq_len(N_sim)) {
		if (verbose && sim %% 10 == 0) {
			cat(sprintf("Viroli noise simulation %d/%d\n", sim, N_sim))
		}

		dataset <- generate_viroli_noise_dataset(n = n, seed = 14000 + sim)
		if (contam_n > 0) {
			tmp <- contaminate_viroli_matrices(dataset$X, n_contam = contam_n, seed = 17000 + sim)
			dataset$X <- tmp$X
			dataset$contam_idx <- tmp$contam_idx
		} else {
			dataset$contam_idx <- integer(0)
		}

		for (method in c("hc", "br")) {
			fit <- matrix_variate_noise_fit(
				x_list = dataset$X,
				g = 3,
				noise_type = method,
				noise_k = 1e-06,
				noise_jitter = 1e-08,
				nstart = 50,
				max_iter = 50,
				verbose = FALSE
			)

			noise_rate <- mean(fit$cluster == 0)
			false_noise_rate <- if (contam_n > 0) {
				clean_idx <- setdiff(seq_len(n), dataset$contam_idx)
				mean(fit$cluster[clean_idx] == 0)
			} else {
				noise_rate
			}
			contam_recall <- if (contam_n > 0) {
				mean(fit$cluster[dataset$contam_idx] == 0)
			} else {
				NA_real_
			}
			cluster_accuracy <- best_three_group_accuracy(fit$cluster, dataset$true_groups)
			ari <- mclust::adjustedRandIndex(fit$cluster, dataset$true_groups)

			results[[result_idx]] <- data.frame(
				sim = sim,
				method = method,
				noise_rate = noise_rate,
				false_noise_rate = false_noise_rate,
				contam_recall = contam_recall,
				cluster_accuracy = cluster_accuracy,
				ari = ari
			)
			result_idx <- result_idx + 1
		}
	}

	results_df <- do.call(rbind, results)
	method_levels <- sort(unique(results_df$method))

	summary_df <- data.frame(
		mean_noise_rate = sapply(method_levels, function(m) mean(results_df$noise_rate[results_df$method == m])),
		sd_noise_rate = sapply(method_levels, function(m) sd(results_df$noise_rate[results_df$method == m])),
		mean_false_noise_rate = sapply(method_levels, function(m) mean(results_df$false_noise_rate[results_df$method == m])),
		mean_contam_recall = sapply(method_levels, function(m) {
			vals <- results_df$contam_recall[results_df$method == m]
			if (all(is.na(vals))) NA_real_ else mean(vals, na.rm = TRUE)
		}),
		mean_cluster_accuracy = sapply(method_levels, function(m) mean(results_df$cluster_accuracy[results_df$method == m], na.rm = TRUE)),
		mean_ari = sapply(method_levels, function(m) mean(results_df$ari[results_df$method == m], na.rm = TRUE))
	)

	rownames(summary_df) <- method_levels

	list(metrics = results_df, summary = summary_df, contam_n = contam_n)
}
```

### Clean Viroli data

```{r}
#| label: viroli_noise_clean_run
#| warning: false
#| message: false

viroli_noise_clean_results <- run_viroli_noise_simulation(
	N_sim = 50,
	n = 100,
	contam_n = 0,
	verbose = TRUE
)

viroli_noise_clean_results$summary
```

```{r}
#| label: viroli_noise_contam_run
#| warning: false
#| message: false

viroli_noise_contam_results <- run_viroli_noise_simulation(
	N_sim = 50,
	n = 100,
	contam_n = 15,
	verbose = TRUE
)

viroli_noise_contam_results$summary
```

## Final Summary Table

```{r}
#| label: noise_final_summary_table
#| warning: false
#| message: false

scenario_summary <- function(results, model_name, scenario_name) {
	df <- as.data.frame(results$summary, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
	if ("sd_noise_rate" %in% names(df)) df$sd_noise_rate <- NULL
	df$method <- rownames(df)
	df$model <- model_name
	df$scenario <- scenario_name
	df <- df[, c("model", "scenario", "method", setdiff(names(df), c("model", "scenario", "method")))]
	rownames(df) <- NULL
	df
}

final_noise_summary <- rbind(
	scenario_summary(tomarchio_noise_clean_results, "Tomarchio", "Clean"),
	scenario_summary(tomarchio_noise_contam_results, "Tomarchio", "Contaminated"),
	scenario_summary(viroli_noise_clean_results, "Viroli", "Clean"),
	scenario_summary(viroli_noise_contam_results, "Viroli", "Contaminated")
)

final_noise_summary
```

HC assumes a simple, element-wise “improper constant k” noise model and treats many individual matrix entries as independent outliers.
Viroli data are clustering by more complex matrix structure (multiple groups, 3×5 matrices, covariance/structural differences and subtle mean shifts), so natural between-group variability looks like element-level noise to HC.
The contamination used for Viroli (randomly permuting entries in whole matrices) is structured, not just extreme single-entry outliers; HC’s element-wise constant-noise assumption fails to model that, so it either (a) flags too many entries as noise (high mean_noise_rate) or (b) collapses cluster structure—both reduce ARI.


(I think this is where the optimized k-finder would be helpful. I guess we could try multiple k's and go from there but whatever.)
Make HC more informative/regularized (tighter prior on k) so it doesn’t absorb structured signal.
Model noise at the matrix level (treat whole matrices as contaminated) or add a structured noise component that can capture permutations.
Increase sample size or use feature-level transforms that remove uninformative variability (e.g., low-rank projection) before applying HC.
